unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 6. Here you learn how to describe people and things with precise words. You collect words for what somebody is like inside, such as kind, honest, friendly and careless, and words for what things are like outside, such as rough, tough, similar and alive. You compare two people with kinder and busier, you tell true stories in the past simple, and you use could to say what somebody was able to do. Along the way you read about Amal, a new girl who discovers that carrying kindness makes everybody feel welcome.
unit.learningPath:
Start with the Vocabulary file and learn a few describing words each day.
Work through the six grammar practices: the past simple, could, Wh- questions, comparatives, present simple statements, and reported speech with imperatives.
Read the two information texts, the two listening texts and the story “The Girl Who Carried Kindness”, then answer the twelve comprehension questions.
Complete the six speaking, six writing and six activity practices.
Join three live teacher sessions in each of the two weeks.
Finish with the ten-question checkpoint and the self-assessment.
unit.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack under docs/lesson-authoring-super-prompt.md

visual.alt: Cartoon children in a school playground, describing each other with kind and precise words.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Nice and helpful to other people.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The kind girl shared her lunch with a new pupil.
practiceSentences: Nora is kind, so she helped the boy who tripped.
Can you think of one kind thing to do today?
“Kind hands help before anybody asks,” said Grandma Hana.
I feel warm inside whenever I am kind to my sister.
Omar the shopkeeper is kind to every customer in the queue.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult sitting beside me, ask me to name two kind acts I noticed this week, then check that I used the word kind in a full sentence.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Happy to talk to people and play with them.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Our new neighbour is very friendly.
practiceSentences: Nora spoke in a friendly voice to the hurt boy.
Are the pupils friendly at your new school?
“Be friendly to anybody who looks lost,” said Teacher Yasmin.
I try to be friendly to every new pupil in our class.
Nadia the bus driver is friendly, and she waves each morning.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: f - r - i - e - n - d - l - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Listen with my adult helper while I greet you three different ways, then tell me which greeting sounded the most friendly.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Always telling the truth, even when it is hard.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Amal is honest, so she told the teacher what really happened.
practiceSentences: An honest pupil handed the lost purse to the school office.
Is it always easy to be honest with your friends?
“An honest heart sleeps peacefully,” said Grandma Hana.
I was honest when I forgot to finish my homework.
Rami the police officer thanked the honest boy who returned the wallet.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: h - o - n - e - s - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With my adult helper listening, give me two short situations and ask me what the honest choice would be in each one.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The act of being kind and helpful to somebody.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She showed kindness by helping the boy who fell.
practiceSentences: One small act of kindness can start many more.
What kindness did you notice in the playground today?
“Kindness is free, so give extra when you can,” said Nora.
My jar of kindness notes is almost full now.
Teacher Yasmin thanked the class for their kindness to Amal.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: k - i - n - d - n - e - s - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me the difference between kind and kindness while my adult helper checks that I gave one example of each.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To look after somebody because you want them to be well.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I care about my little brother.
practiceSentences: Doctor Sarah takes great care of everybody at the clinic.
Who will care for the class fish this weekend?
“I care for my plants every single morning,” said Maya.
I care about my friends when they feel sad.
Amal wrote her description with care and checked every word.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, ask me who I care for at home and how I show it, then check that my answer is a complete sentence.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Doing many things and having little free time.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The teacher was too busy to talk right now.
practiceSentences: The market is busy on Saturday morning.
Why is our kitchen so busy this evening?
“I am busy now, but ask me again later,” said Dad.
I keep busy with reading and drawing on rainy days.
Nora was always busy helping somebody in our class.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to describe my busiest day of the week while my adult helper checks that I used busy correctly twice.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Quiet and gentle, and not angry or excited.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Nora stayed calm and helped in a soft voice.
practiceSentences: The sea looked calm and flat this morning.
How do you stay calm before a spelling test?
“Take one calm, deep breath first,” said Teacher Yasmin.
I feel calm when I read a book before bedtime.
Leila the firefighter stayed calm while the smoke alarm rang.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With my adult helper beside me, ask me three questions in a calm voice and let me copy that calm tone in my answers.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Having the skill or the strength to do something.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I am able to tie my own shoes now.
practiceSentences: Maya is able to swim right across the pool.
Are you able to reach the top shelf?
“I am able to ride without help now,” said Sami.
I am able to spell every Unit 6 word correctly.
Karim is able to clean the highest windows safely.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me three questions that begin with are you able to, and let my adult helper confirm that I answered in full sentences.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Not careful, so you make mistakes or break things.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: A careless move knocked the cup off the table.
practiceSentences: A careless elbow knocked the paint pot over.
Was it careless to leave the garden gate open?
“Careless rushing breaks things, so slow down,” said Mum.
I was careless once and lost my new pencil.
Amal wrote that she wanted to grow stronger, not careless.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - a - r - e - l - e - s - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult nearby, ask me to use careful and careless in the same short story so you can hear the difference.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Liked and chosen by many different people.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: He is popular at school because he is kind to everyone.
practiceSentences: The reading corner is popular during rainy breaks.
Why is that puppet show so popular with young children?
“Football is popular at our school,” said Leo.
Grandma Hana's soup is popular with our whole family.
Nora was popular, but she never showed off.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - o - p - u - l - a - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me which game is popular in my playground and why, then check with my adult helper that I gave one clear reason.

dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The road was very rough after the heavy rain.
practiceSentences: Tree bark feels rough under your fingers.
Is the path too rough for a bicycle?
“The rough sea rocked the little boat,” said Theo.
I smoothed the rough wood with a piece of sandpaper.
A rough wind blew the class papers across the garden.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With my adult helper watching, ask me to touch two surfaces at home and say which one is rough and why.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Strong and hard to break, tear or hurt.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My boots are tough, so they are good for walking far.
practiceSentences: This bag is tough, so it never tears.
Are your new shoes tough enough for the rough path?
“My uncle is tough, but he helps everyone,” said Daniel.
I chose a tough cover for my reading book.
Leila needs tough gloves and a tough helmet for her work.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to explain the difference between rough and tough, and have my adult helper check that I gave one example of each.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Almost the same as something else, but not exactly.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My bag is similar to yours because they are both blue.
practiceSentences: These two shells look similar, but one is larger.
Is your handwriting similar to your brother's handwriting?
“Our lunchboxes are similar,” said Mina with a laugh.
I think Amal and Nora are similar in many ways.
The two roads near my home are not similar at all.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - i - m - i - l - a - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, ask me to find two similar objects in this room and to say one way they are still different.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Living and growing, and not dead.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The little fish is still alive in the bucket of water.
practiceSentences: The seed we planted is alive and growing fast.
Is that beetle alive, or is it only sleeping?
“Every plant in this garden is alive,” said Grandma Hana.
I feel more alive when I run in the fresh air.
Amal said she felt alive after making a new friend.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to name three things in my home that are alive and three that are not, while my adult helper listens.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The one you like the most of all.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My favourite food is rice and beans.
practiceSentences: Amal's favourite person carries kindness every single day.
What is your favourite colour, and why do you like it?
“My favourite person is my uncle,” said Daniel.
I read my favourite story again last night.
The reading corner is my favourite place in our classroom.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: f - a - v - o - u - r - i - t - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With my adult helper beside me, ask me about my favourite food, colour and game, then check that I answered in full sentences.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: More than you need, or more than usual.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We brought extra pencils for the class.
practiceSentences: Nora's mother says to give extra kindness when you can.
Do you need an extra chair for your visitor?
“Sometimes we forget the extra good we do,” said Teacher Yasmin.
I packed an extra jumper because the wind was cold.
Omar gave us an extra orange for being polite.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me when having something extra is helpful and when it is wasteful, with my adult helper joining the discussion.

rubrics.level4: Uses three or more unit adjectives accurately
Responds, asks a Wh- question and extends the idea
Ideas connect smoothly with and, but and because
Precise adjectives and one accurate comparative

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and full stops need support
rubrics.level4: Consistently correct capitals, full stops and spelling

outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Ten Unit 6 adjectives used correctly in Writing 3 — Sort the Traits and in the Speaking 2 object description.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Describe your favourite person and say what makes them special.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: A four-sentence favourite-person description in Writing 1 and a clear Speaking 1 recording.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Compare two people or things, using words like kinder and rougher than.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Two accurate comparative sentences in Writing 2, plus correct answers to the Grammar 4 practice.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Talk about the past, using was, were and verbs like helped and went.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Correct past-simple forms in the Grammar 1 practice and Activity 3, and past-tense verbs in Writing 4.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Use the word could to talk about what someone was able to do.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Correct use of could in the Grammar 2 practice and an accurate answer to comprehension question 10.

outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Three well-formed Wh- questions asked during the Speaking 3 Guess Who game.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Write a short description of a person, using at least three adjectives.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: A finished portfolio description that uses three unit adjectives and one reason introduced by because.

readings.title: My Cousin Noah
readings.setting: Familiar home, school and town settings
readings.passageScript: My cousin Noah is my favourite person. He is kind and honest. He is always busy, but he still has time to help me. When I feel worried, he stays calm and speaks in a friendly voice. Noah is popular at school because he cares about everyone.
readings.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack

readings.title: Two Roads
readings.setting: Familiar home, school and town settings
readings.passageScript: There are two roads near my home. The first road is smooth and easy to walk on. The second road is rough, with many bumps and stones. My father says his old boots are tough, so the rough road is not a problem for him. The two roads are not similar at all!
readings.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack

readings.title: Describing a Friend
readings.setting: Familiar home, school and town settings
readings.passageScript: “My best friend at school is Nora. She is very kind and friendly to everybody. Yesterday, I was sad, but Nora was calm and she helped me. She is honest too — she always tells the truth. Nora is my favourite friend of all!”
readings.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack

readings.title: Who Is Kinder?
readings.setting: Familiar home, school and town settings
readings.passageScript: “My brother is kind to everybody,” said Sami. “But my brother is kinder than yours!” said Leo. “He is friendlier than anybody else in our street. He could carry ten bags of rice when he was only twelve!”
readings.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack

readings.title: The Girl Who Carried Kindness
readings.setting: Familiar home, school and town settings
readings.passageScript:
The school bell rang, and the noisy playground filled with children.
They laughed, ran, shouted and played football together.
But in one corner, Amal sat alone and held her lunchbox.
She was new, because her family had moved to the town that term.
Nobody in Grade 3 knew her name yet.
That week, Teacher Yasmin gave the class a writing task.
“Describe your favourite person,” she said.
“Can it be somebody from our school?” asked Daniel.
“Yes,” said Teacher Yasmin. “Write with care. Be honest. Use adjectives.”
Amal thought for a long moment, but she had no favourite person yet.
The next day, Amal saw a girl pick up a fallen bag.
The girl ran after its owner and handed the bag back politely.
“She could have ignored it,” said another pupil.
“But she did not,” whispered Amal. “She really cared about that girl.”
The girl’s name was Nora, the most popular pupil in our class.
She was clever, kind, and always busy helping somebody.
One afternoon the whole class went outside to the school garden.
A rough wind blew, and the loose papers flew everywhere.
“Somebody catch them!” shouted Teacher Yasmin.
Most pupils chased the papers across the grass, laughing loudly.
But Amal saw a boy trip over a chair, and he looked hurt.
Nora did not laugh, but stopped at once and helped him sit up.
“You will be fine,” she said in a calm, friendly voice.
Amal watched, and she saw that Nora was strong, tough and gentle.
That night, Amal wrote three sentences in her notebook:
“She was kind to the boy who fell over in the garden.
She was friendly to the girl who dropped her heavy bag.
She could have laughed at them, but instead she showed care.”
The next morning, Amal found the courage to speak to Nora.
“Hello,” she said shyly. “I saw you help other people, and that was good.”
“Thank you,” said Nora with a warm and open smile.
“My mother says kindness is free, so give extra when you can.”
Amal smiled too, and the lonely feeling grew a little smaller.
Teacher Yasmin gave a new task the following day.
“Compare two people in our class today.
Who is more helpful, and who is rougher?”
Some pupils wrote, “Daniel is rougher than Nora.”
Others wrote, “Nora is always calm and helpful to everybody.”
At the end of the week, the class shared their descriptions aloud.
Daniel said, “My favourite person is my uncle.
He is tough, but he helps everyone on our street.”
Then Amal stood up in front of the whole class.
“My favourite person carries kindness every single day.
She does not show off, and she simply helps.”
“Who is it?” asked Teacher Yasmin.
Amal pointed at Nora and began to explain.
“She gave back a fallen bag, and she helped a hurt boy.
And she gave me a smile on my very first day.”
“Nobody has ever told me that,” said Nora, looking very surprised.
Teacher Yasmin smiled at them both.
“Sometimes we forget the extra good that we do.”
Later that day, Nora and Amal sat together under the tall tree.
“You know,” said Nora, “you are kind too.
You noticed the things that other people missed.”
“I used to feel alone, but now I feel more alive,” laughed Amal.
Teacher Yasmin told the class, “Describe somebody again.
This time, include yourself, because you matter too.”
Amal wrote her last description that evening:
“I am similar to Nora, and I want to care for people.
I want to grow stronger and braver, not careless.
I am able to give kindness every day, because I choose to.”
readings.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack

comprehension.question: Who is the writer's favourite person in ‘My Cousin Noah’?
comprehension.correctAnswer: The writer's favourite person is her cousin Noah.
comprehension.explanation: The opening sentence names Noah, so this is a straight recall of the first line.
comprehension.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack

comprehension.question: Why is Noah popular at school?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Noah is popular at school because he cares about everyone.
comprehension.explanation: The final sentence gives the reason, and the word because points straight to it.
comprehension.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack

comprehension.question: Which three adjectives does the writer use to describe Noah?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Any three of these words: kind, honest, busy, calm and friendly.
comprehension.explanation: Each adjective sits beside Noah in the text, so the learner can underline the exact words.
comprehension.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack

comprehension.question: What is the first road like, and what is the second road like?
comprehension.correctAnswer: The first road is smooth and easy to walk on, and the second road is rough with bumps and stones.
comprehension.explanation: The two middle sentences contrast the smooth road with the rough one, so both halves are needed.
comprehension.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack

comprehension.question: Why is the rough road not a problem for the writer's father?
comprehension.correctAnswer: His old boots are tough, so the bumps and the stones do not trouble his feet.
comprehension.explanation: The word so links the tough boots to the rough road, which is the cause the learner must spot.
comprehension.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack

comprehension.question: Are the two roads similar, and which words in the text tell you?
comprehension.correctAnswer: They are not similar, and the last line says they are not similar at all.
comprehension.explanation: The closing exclamation states the difference outright, so the learner quotes it as evidence.
comprehension.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack

comprehension.question: What is the name of the best friend in ‘Describing a Friend’?
comprehension.correctAnswer: The speaker's best friend is Nora.
comprehension.explanation: The first spoken line names Nora, and every later sentence keeps describing the same person.
comprehension.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack

comprehension.question: How did Nora help the speaker yesterday?
comprehension.correctAnswer: The speaker was sad, and Nora stayed calm and helped her.
comprehension.explanation: Two past-tense verbs, was and helped, carry the answer and show the event is finished.
comprehension.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack

comprehension.question: Which two words ending in -er compare the brothers in ‘Who Is Kinder?’
comprehension.correctAnswer: The comparing words are kinder and friendlier.
comprehension.explanation: Both words add -er to a short adjective, which is how Grade 3 compares exactly two people.
comprehension.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack

comprehension.question: What could Leo's brother do when he was only twelve?
comprehension.correctAnswer: He could carry ten bags of rice.
comprehension.explanation: The word could signals a past ability, and the sentence states exactly what that ability was.
comprehension.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack

comprehension.question: Why was Amal sitting alone when the story begins?
comprehension.correctAnswer: She was new, because her family had moved to the town, so nobody knew her name yet.
comprehension.explanation: The fourth and fifth lines explain her loneliness, and both are needed for a full answer.
comprehension.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack

comprehension.question: How did Amal’s feelings change between the playground and the tall tree?
comprehension.correctAnswer: She felt alone in the playground, and under the tree she felt more alive.
comprehension.explanation: Amal names both feelings herself, so the evidence is in her own words rather than in a guess.
comprehension.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack

grammar.title: The Past Simple Tense
grammar.explanation: We use the past simple to talk about things that already happened. It helps us tell stories and describe what people did, like Nora, who helped the boy who fell.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Most verbs simply add -ed: play → played, help → helped, walk → walked.
Some verbs are irregular and change completely: go → went, have → had, see → saw.
The verb to be changes too: is and am become was, and are becomes were.
Examples: “She was kind to her classmate.” · “They played outside yesterday.” · “He went to the shop.”
grammar.commonMistake: Children often write “He goed to the shop yesterday.” The verb go is irregular, so its past simple is went: “He went to the shop yesterday.”
grammar.memoryTip: The past simple is a time machine. When you use it, you travel back to something that has already finished.
grammar.practice: Write the past simple form of the verb in brackets. | Last week, I ______ (visit) my grandmother. | She ______ (finish) her homework early. | They ______ (play) football at the park. | He ______ (go) to the shop yesterday.
grammar.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack

grammar.title: The Word ‘Could’
grammar.explanation: Could is a helper verb. We use it for things that were possible in the past, and for asking politely.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Past ability: “When I was five, I could swim.”
Something possible: “They could be calm even on rough days.”
Polite request: “Could you lend me your pencil, please?”
Always use the plain verb after could: could play, could be, could help.
grammar.commonMistake: Some children write “He could plays football.” Could never lets the next verb take an -s: “He could play football.”
grammar.memoryTip: Could is a time traveller. It reports what somebody was able to do before, or what might still happen.
grammar.practice: Fill in each blank using could and the verb in brackets. | When I was little, I ______ (ride) my bike without help. | If we practise every day, we ______ (be) champions! | ______ you please help me with my project?
grammar.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack

grammar.title: Wh- Questions
grammar.explanation: Wh- questions begin with who, what, when, where, why or how. They ask for exactly the information you want, and good learners ask plenty of them.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
The order is: Wh- word, then verb, then subject.
Who asks about a person: “Who is your best friend?”
What asks about a thing: “What makes somebody honest?”
Why asks for a reason: “Why is he so friendly?”
How asks about the way: “How do you get to school?”
grammar.commonMistake: A common slip is “Why he is friendly?” The verb belongs before the subject: “Why is he friendly?”
grammar.memoryTip: The Wh- words are your question detectives: who, what, when, where, why and how.
grammar.practice: Choose the correct Wh- word. | ______ is your favourite colour? (What / Where) | ______ do you go to bed? (When / How) | ______ is your best friend? (Who / Why) | ______ do you like the story? (How / Why)
grammar.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack

grammar.title: Comparing Two Things
grammar.explanation: A comparative compares exactly two things. It lets you say that one person is kinder, busier or rougher than another.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Short words add -er: tall → taller, rough → rougher.
Words ending in -y change the y to i, then add -er: happy → happier, busy → busier.
Longer words use more: helpful → more helpful, careful → more careful.
Then add than: “My dog is bigger than my cat.”
A few words are irregular: good → better, bad → worse.
grammar.commonMistake: Children sometimes write “My dog is more big than my cat.” Short adjectives take -er instead: “My dog is bigger than my cat.”
grammar.memoryTip: Think of -er as a small comparison badge you clip onto short adjectives. Longer adjectives borrow the word more instead.
grammar.practice: Write the comparative form of the word in brackets. | This puzzle is ______ (easy) than that one. | She is ______ (smart) than her brother. | This story is ______ (funny) than the last one.
grammar.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack

grammar.title: Present Simple Statements
grammar.explanation: We use the present simple to describe people and things, and to say what is true right now. It is perfect for describing a friend.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use is for one person or thing: “My friend is kind and careful.”
Use are for more than one: “Popular pupils are not always kind.”
With he, she and it, action verbs add -s: “She plays the piano.” · “He helps everyone.”
grammar.commonMistake: A frequent slip is “He play football.” Add the -s after he, she or it: “He plays football.”
grammar.memoryTip: Say “s for singular”. If the subject is he, she or it, the action verb needs its own -s.
grammar.practice: Fill in each blank with the correct form of the verb in brackets. | My sister ______ (like) to read books. | The dogs ______ (be) very friendly. | My parents ______ (enjoy) cooking together. | He ______ (help) his friends every day.
grammar.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack

grammar.title: Telling What Someone Said + Imperatives (Giving Instructions)
grammar.explanation: Reported speech tells somebody what another person said, without using their exact words. An imperative gives a clear instruction, and this unit uses both.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Reported speech starts with a reporting word: said, told or asked.
Move the verb one step back in time: “I am happy” becomes “She said she was happy.”
Change the pronoun to match: “My cousin is busy” becomes “She said her cousin was busy.”
An imperative starts with the plain verb and needs no subject: “Describe your best friend.”
Use clear action words such as List, Write, Draw and Explain, and add please to sound polite.
For a negative instruction, begin with Do not or Don’t: “Don’t forget your adjectives.”
grammar.commonMistake: Two slips are common here. “She said her cousin is busy” keeps the present tense, so write “She said her cousin was busy.” “You describe your favourite classmate” keeps the subject, so write “Describe your favourite classmate.”
grammar.memoryTip: For reported speech, chant “change the tense, change the pronoun”. For an imperative, point at your friend and say the action straight out.
grammar.practice: Part A. Finish each reported sentence. | Direct: “I love ice cream.” → Reported: He said he ______ ice cream. | Direct: “Where are you going?” → Reported: She asked where I ______ going. | Direct: “Don’t touch that!” → Reported: He told me ______ touch that. | Part B. Choose one verb from the box: Describe, List, Draw, Write. | ______ your favourite animal. | ______ three things you love about school. | ______ a picture of your family. | ______ words that describe a good friend.
grammar.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack

speaking.title: Speaking 1 — Describe Your Favourite Person
speaking.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Get ready to say three or four sentences about somebody you admire. Use these starters:
“My favourite person is ______.”
“He or she is ______ and ______.” (choose two adjectives from this unit)
“He or she helps me when ______.”
Record yourself, then check three things: did you use two adjectives, did you say why you like the person, and did you speak clearly?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside me, listen to my favourite-person description and tell me whether my two adjectives were precise enough.

speaking.title: Speaking 2 — Describe a Thing
speaking.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Pick up an object near you and describe it in two or three sentences:
“This is my ______.”
“It feels ______.” (for example rough, smooth, tough or heavy)
“It is similar to a / an ______.”
Say each sentence twice, once slowly and once at your normal speed.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult listening, ask me to describe the object in my hand using rough, tough or similar, then check my word choice.

speaking.title: Speaking 3 — Guess Who
speaking.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Play this game with your AI tutor or your family.
Think of a person everybody knows, but keep the name secret.
Describe the person with three adjectives: “This person is kind, busy and honest.”
The other player asks Wh- questions, such as “Who does this person help?” or “Why do you like them?”
Keep going until the person is guessed, and then swap turns.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult in the room, play Guess Who with me and ask one Wh- question after every clue I give.

speaking.title: Speaking 4 — Vocabulary Expert Interview
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Choose six words from this unit, such as kindness, careless, tough and alive. Ask your partner one question about each word, and answer their questions in complete sentences. For example: “What does careless mean, and when were you careless?”
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult nearby, interview me about six Unit 6 words and tell me if my meanings match the ones in my word book.

speaking.title: Speaking 5 — Explain and Respond
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Explain why Nora is kinder than she believes she is. Give one reason using because, then answer two follow-up questions from your listener without stopping.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, ask me two follow-up questions after I compare two people, and check that I used a comparative with than.

speaking.title: Speaking 6 — One-Minute Unit Talk
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Plan and record a one-minute talk called “The Person Who Carries Kindness”. Use an opening sentence, three connected details with adjectives, and a closing sentence about yourself.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult supervising, time my one-minute talk and tell me which of my three details needed a stronger adjective.

writing.title: Writing 1 — A Description of a Person
writing.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write four sentences about a person you like. Use at least three adjectives from this unit. You can begin:
My favourite person is ______.
He or she is ______, ______ and ______.
He or she is good at ______.
I like this person because ______.
writing.modelText: My favourite person is my cousin Noah. He is kind, honest and very calm. He is good at explaining hard words to me. I like this person because he never makes me feel small.
writing.successCriteria: I named the person, used three unit adjectives, gave one reason with because, and checked every capital letter and full stop.
writing.support: Say all four sentences aloud first, then write down the two you liked best.
writing.extension: Add one comparative sentence showing how this person is kinder or busier than somebody else.

writing.title: Writing 2 — Comparing Two People
writing.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write two sentences that compare two people you know. Use the comparatives from Grammar 4:
“______ is kinder than ______.”
“______ is busier than ______.”
Then write one more sentence that explains why you chose those two words.
writing.modelText: Nora is kinder than she believes. Adam is busier than Idris on football days. I chose kind and busy because they are short adjectives that simply take -er.
writing.successCriteria: I compared exactly two people, spelled each -er ending correctly, used than in both sentences, and explained one choice.
writing.support: Write the two adjectives first, add -er to each, and only then build the sentence around them.
writing.extension: Write a third sentence with more, such as more helpful or more careful, to compare a longer adjective.

writing.title: Writing 3 — Sort the Traits
writing.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack
writing.promptAndInstructions: Make two lists. In the first list, write describing words for how somebody acts or feels, such as kind, honest and calm. In the second list, write describing words for how a thing looks or feels, such as rough, tough and smooth. Check both lists with your AI tutor while an adult is nearby.
writing.modelText: People words: kind, honest, calm, friendly, busy, careless. Thing words: rough, tough, smooth, similar, alive, heavy. The word tough can join both lists, because a person can be tough as well.
writing.sentenceStarter: The word kind belongs in the people list because
writing.successCriteria: I sorted at least ten unit words, placed each word in only one list, and explained one word that could belong to both.
writing.support: Ask one question for every word: can I touch this quality, or does it live inside a person?
writing.extension: Add three describing words from your own reading, and say which list each one joins.

writing.title: Writing 4 — A Kindness Paragraph
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a six-sentence paragraph about a time somebody was kind to you. Begin with a topic sentence, keep every verb in the past simple, and join your ideas with and, but or because.
writing.modelText: Last winter, a neighbour was very kind to my family. Our gate broke, and nobody could open it. Karim came with his tools, but he refused any payment. He worked for an hour because he wanted my little sister to reach school on time. His kindness was quiet, and nobody else noticed it. I still think about that cold morning.
writing.sentenceStarter: Last winter, somebody was kind to my family because
writing.successCriteria: I opened with a topic sentence, joined ideas with and, but and because, and kept every verb in the past simple.
writing.support: Plan three moments on scrap paper, then turn each moment into two sentences.
writing.extension: Rewrite two sentences so they open with a time phrase, such as Later that day.

writing.title: Writing 5 — A Fact File About a Person
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a heading and five true facts about somebody in your family. Use precise unit vocabulary, and check your spelling and punctuation before you finish.
writing.modelText: Facts about my grandmother Hana. She is the calmest person in our house. She is able to bake bread without a recipe. She was a teacher for thirty years. Her hands are rough, but her voice is gentle. She is popular with every child on our street.
writing.successCriteria: I wrote a clear heading, listed five separate facts, used four unit words, and proofread for capital letters and full stops.
writing.support: Number your five facts before writing, so that no fact is repeated.
writing.extension: Add one fact written in the past simple, and underline the past-tense verb.

writing.title: Writing 6 — Reflect and Improve
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Choose one piece of writing from this unit. Revise three sentences so the adjectives are more precise, add two useful details, and write one sentence explaining the best improvement you made.
writing.modelText: I changed “She is nice” to “She is calm and honest”, because nice tells the reader almost nothing. I added the detail about her rough hands and her gentle voice. My best improvement was the comparative sentence, because it shows two people instead of one.
writing.successCriteria: I replaced three weak adjectives, added two details, explained one improvement, and reread the whole piece aloud.
writing.support: Circle every adjective in your draft, then ask whether a Unit 6 word would say it better.
writing.extension: Swap drafts with a partner and suggest one precise adjective for their writing.

activities.title: Activities 1 — Choose the Right Word
activities.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Choose the best describing word for each sentence.
1. The rock was hard and ______ to break. (tough / calm)
2. She always tells the truth, so she is very ______. (honest / rough)
3. He has many things to do today, so he is ______. (busy / alive)
4. My shirt looks almost the same as yours, so they are ______. (similar / careless)

activities.title: Activities 2 — Opposites
activities.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Match each word to its opposite.
1. careful → ______ (careless / kind)
2. rough → ______ (smooth / busy)
3. dead → ______ (alive / tough)
4. unkind → ______ (kind / rough)

activities.title: Activities 3 — Past or Now?
activities.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Fill in each blank with is (now) or was (in the past).
1. Right now, my sister ______ busy with her homework.
2. Yesterday, Nora ______ kind to the new boy.
3. Today the road ______ rough after the rain.
4. Last week, Teacher Yasmin ______ happy with our writing.

activities.title: Activities 4 — Describe and Draw
activities.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack
activities.instructionsAndItems: Draw a person from your family. Around your drawing, write four adjectives that describe them. Read your four words aloud to your AI tutor or your teacher, and add one spoken sentence for each word.
activities.answerSummary: Four different adjectives around the drawing, plus four spoken sentences.

activities.title: Activities 5 — Vocabulary Sort and Explain
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Sort twelve unit words into three groups: kind-person words, how-people-act words, and thing words. Write one sentence that explains the rule for each group, and name the word that was hardest to place.
activities.answerSummary: Three labelled groups, three rule sentences, and one borderline word named.

activities.title: Activities 6 — Story Evidence Hunt
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Find six details in “The Girl Who Carried Kindness”. For each detail, copy the sentence from the story and explain what it shows about Nora or about Amal. One of your six must be the sentence where Nora repeats what her mother says about kindness.
activities.answerSummary: Six copied sentences, each with an explanation, including the mother's line about kindness.

assignments.title: Describing People and Things learning portfolio
assignments.instructions:
Submit two pieces of work. First, Writing 1 — A Description of a Person, using at least three adjectives from this unit. Second, a recording of Speaking 1 — Describe Your Favourite Person. Improve both pieces with the Grade 3 writing and speaking rubrics before you send them.
Marking guidance for the teacher: award up to 16 marks for the writing, using the Ideas, Organization, Describing language and Conventions criteria, and up to 16 marks for the recording, using the Clarity, Adjective range, Sentence accuracy and Interaction criteria. Award full Describing language marks only when three different unit adjectives are used correctly. Award full Clarity marks only when the whole recording can be understood on a single listen. Where the child names a real person, accept any respectful description and mark the language, not the choice of person.
assignments.sourceFile: Derived from unit writing and speaking tasks

quizzes.question: What does the word kindness mean?
quizzes.options: The act of being kind and helpful to somebody. | A rough road with many bumps, stones and puddles. | A person who is liked by many other people. | Something you are able to do without any help.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The act of being kind and helpful to somebody.
quizzes.explanation: Kindness names the action, while kind is the adjective that describes the person doing it.

quizzes.question: Which meaning matches the adjective tough?
quizzes.options: Almost the same as something else, but not exactly. | Strong and hard to break, tear or hurt. | Living and growing, and not dead. | Nice and helpful to other people.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Strong and hard to break, tear or hurt.
quizzes.explanation: Tough describes strength, so boots and gloves are tough, while rough describes a bumpy surface.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from ‘My Cousin Noah’: He is always _____, but he still has time to help me.
quizzes.explanation: The text says Noah is always busy, and the word but shows the surprise that he still finds time.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from ‘Two Roads’: My father says his old boots are _____, so the rough road is not a problem.
quizzes.explanation: Only tough describes an object, because honest, popular and calm all describe people.

quizzes.question: In the story, which two kind acts did Amal write about in her notebook?
quizzes.options: Nora caught the papers and closed the gate. | Nora gave back a fallen bag and helped a boy who fell. | Nora carried ten heavy bags of rice and cleaned every window. | Nora shared her lunch and mended a broken chair.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Nora gave back a fallen bag and helped a boy who fell.
quizzes.explanation: Amal's notebook names the fallen bag and the boy who tripped in the garden, and nothing else.

quizzes.question: Which sentence uses the past simple correctly?
quizzes.options: He goed to the shop yesterday. | He gone to the shop yesterday afternoon. | He went to the shop yesterday. | He go to the shop yesterday.
quizzes.correctAnswer: He went to the shop yesterday.
quizzes.explanation: Go is an irregular verb, so its past simple is went rather than goed or gone.

quizzes.question: What is the correct comparative form of the adjective happy?
quizzes.explanation: Words ending in -y change the y to i before -er, so happy becomes happier.

quizzes.question: Which sentence uses could correctly?
quizzes.options: She could plays the drum. | She could to play the drum. | She could played the drum. | She could play the drum.
quizzes.explanation: Could is always followed by the plain verb, so play takes no -s, no -ed and no to.

quizzes.question: Teacher Yasmin wants to know the reason for something. Which question word should she use?
quizzes.explanation: Why asks for a reason, where asks about a place, when asks about a time and who asks about a person.

quizzes.question: Nora said, “I am busy.” Which sentence reports her words correctly?
quizzes.options: Nora said she is busy. | Nora said her busy. | Nora said she was busy. | Nora said, she busy today.
quizzes.explanation: Reported speech moves the verb one step back, so am becomes was and the speech marks disappear.

liveSessions.title: Describing People and Things: Launch and the kind-person words
liveSessions.beforeSession: Read the unit overview and learn the five words in Group 1.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min welcome and word wall; 10 min teacher models kind, friendly, honest, kindness and care; 15 min partner sorting of the five words; 10 min sentence building with each word; 5 min goal setting
liveSessions.afterSession: Write one sentence for each Group 1 word and bring it to the next session.

liveSessions.title: Describing People and Things: Reading the two information texts
liveSessions.beforeSession: Read ‘My Cousin Noah’ and ‘Two Roads’ aloud twice, and mark every adjective.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min fluency warm-up; 10 min shared reading of My Cousin Noah; 15 min evidence hunt in Two Roads; 10 min answering comprehension questions in full sentences; 5 min review of rough against tough
liveSessions.afterSession: Answer comprehension questions 4 to 6 in complete sentences.

liveSessions.title: Describing People and Things: Past simple and the word could
liveSessions.beforeSession: Complete the Grammar 1 and Grammar 2 practices and bring your answers.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min retrieval of -ed endings; 10 min teacher models went, had and saw; 15 min guided past-simple practice; 10 min paired work on could for past ability; 5 min feedback on irregular verbs
liveSessions.afterSession: Correct your Grammar 1 and Grammar 2 answers, and note two irregular verbs to learn.

liveSessions.title: Describing People and Things: Comparatives and the Guess Who game
liveSessions.beforeSession: Complete the Grammar 4 practice and choose two people you could compare.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min sorting -er against more; 10 min teacher models kinder, busier and more helpful; 15 min comparing pairs of classroom objects; 10 min Guess Who with Wh- questions; 5 min reflection on turn taking
liveSessions.afterSession: Record Speaking 3 and note one Wh- question that worked well.

liveSessions.title: Describing People and Things: Story reading and character evidence
liveSessions.beforeSession: Read “The Girl Who Carried Kindness” and mark three sentences about Nora.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min prediction from the title; 10 min shared reading of the garden scene; 15 min collecting evidence about Nora and Amal; 10 min drafting notebook sentences; 5 min sharing favourite lines
liveSessions.afterSession: Complete Activity 6 and bring your six copied sentences.

liveSessions.title: Describing People and Things: Portfolio, checkpoint and reflection
liveSessions.beforeSession: Draft Writing 1 and record Speaking 1 before the session.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min rubric walk-through; 10 min teacher models a strong description; 15 min peer feedback on adjective choices; 10 min revision time; 5 min setting one improvement goal
liveSessions.afterSession: Submit the portfolio, then complete the checkpoint quiz and the self-assessment.

teacherNotes.note: Model every new adjective aloud and check meaning before independent work. An adult stays with the child for each AI tutor task, and no child is left alone with the device or the microphone. Give specific feedback on complete sentences, on text evidence and on adjective precision, and praise the effort rather than the speed. When a child describes a real person, mark the language and never the family they chose.

Ask Your AI Tutor — from the Lesson file
“Can you read ‘My Cousin Noah’ with me and explain the describing words?”
“Can you play a listening game where you describe a person and I guess the adjectives?”
“Can you help me practise describing my favourite person?”
“Can you play Guess Who with me?”
“Did I use my adjectives correctly?”
“Can you check my description and tell me what to fix?”
“Can you help me make my comparing sentences correct?”
Ask Your AI Tutor — from the Vocabulary file
“Can you help me describe a kind person?”
“What is the difference between kind and kindness?”
“Can you give me other words that mean friendly?”
“Can you quiz me on these five words?”
“Can you help me use careless and careful in sentences?”
“What is the difference between rough and tough?”
“Can you give me two things that are similar so I can compare them?”
“Can you ask me about my favourite things?”
“In the story, somebody says give extra when you can. What does that mean?”
Ask Your AI Tutor — from the Grammar file
“Can you check my grammar answers?”
“Why do we say she was kind and not she is kind when we tell a story about yesterday?”
“Can you give me five more sentences to practise comparatives?”
Ask Your AI Tutor — from the Story file
“Can you read the story with me and explain any hard words?”
“Can you check my writing about my favourite person?”
“Can you ask me questions about the story to see if I understood it?”
Every one of these prompts is used with an adult in the room.
teacherNotes.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: 1. tough 2. honest 3. busy 4. similar. Tough is the only option that says how hard a rock is to break; calm describes a person.
answerKey.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: 1. careless 2. smooth 3. alive 4. kind. Show the child that careless is simply careful with the ending -less, meaning without care.
answerKey.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: 1. is 2. was 3. is 4. was. The time words right now, yesterday, today and last week decide the tense every single time.
answerKey.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Expect four different adjectives written around the drawing and four spoken sentences. At least two of the four words should come from the Unit 6 word wall.
answerKey.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Kind-person words: kind, friendly, honest, kindness, care. How-people-act words: busy, calm, able, careless, popular. Thing words: rough, tough, similar, alive. Tough is the fair borderline case, because a person can be tough too.
answerKey.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Expect six sentences copied from the story, each with an explanation. The mother's line is “Kindness is free, so give extra when you can,” which shows where Nora learned to help.
answerKey.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: The act of being kind and helpful to somebody. Kindness names the action; kind names the person who does it.
answerKey.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Strong and hard to break, tear or hurt. Boots, gloves and bags are tough, while a road is rough instead.
answerKey.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: busy. The text reads: He is always busy, but he still has time to help me.
answerKey.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: tough. The text reads: My father says his old boots are tough, so the rough road is not a problem for him.
answerKey.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Nora gave back a fallen bag and helped a boy who fell. Amal listed exactly these two acts in her notebook that night.
answerKey.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: He went to the shop yesterday. Go is irregular, so it never takes the -ed ending.
answerKey.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: happier. Change the final y to i, and then add -er.
answerKey.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: She could play the drum. Could is always followed by the plain verb.
answerKey.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Why. It is the only Wh- word in the list that asks for a reason.
answerKey.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Nora said she was busy. The verb moves back one step in time and the speech marks are removed.
answerKey.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 3 Unit 6 source pack
